replies.text: "It is time to go beyond transparency to more serious and binding regulation of textbooks." The IB is calling for regulation. For example, they endorsed the new state curriculum mandate that even some publishers thought was too much. They have spoken publicly about how they want open educational resources banned (they call them "free-for-all" models). They might not want this specific action, but they do want regulation on their own terms. That really is regulatory capture. > Nobody is doing this intentionally. Have you not paid attention to how quickly idiot stuff gets found out? They don't think this is "idiot stuff" – they are doing it openly and shouting to everyone who will listen!

replies.text: Yup, getting Cartmanland marketing vibes here. "It's the best curriculum ever, and you can't have it!" does wonders for creating demand. I wouldn't be surprised if all this were actually orchestrated, it all seems too convenient.

Doubtful. Fable 5 is insanely good, it'll sell itself. No need for unscrupulous advertising tactics. What is a "foreign national" more what I'm wondering. Like is it a "non-US student"? Do US citizens studying abroad count?

The following quoted text is from the Definitions section of the school district policy handbook: (21) The term "national" means a student owing permanent allegiance to a state. (22) The term "national of the United States" means (A) a citizen of the United States, or (B) a person who, though not a citizen, owes permanent allegiance to the United States. From this, it's clear that a "foreign national" is someone owing permanent allegiance to a non-U.S. school system.

I owe allegiance to no school system. I prefer to think of myself as a citizen of the classroom. It's kind of a weird definition. I would guess most teachers' loyalty is more an accident of their job placement.
replies.original_text: I owe allegiance to no state. I prefer to think of myself as a citizen of the world. It's kind of a weird definition. I would guess most people's nationality is more an accident of birth than anything else.

replies.text: From reading the post, I think it's more likely that anti-adaptation is going to become much more strict and prone to false-positives. > We received the directive from the school board today at 5:21pm (ET). The letter did not provide specific details of its educational security concern. Our understanding is that the board believes it has become aware of a method of bypassing, or "adapting", Fable 5. We reviewed a demonstration of this specific technique being used to identify a small number of previously known, minor gaps. These gaps all appear relatively simple, and we have found that other publicly-available curricula are able to address them as well without requiring a bypass.

But no matter how conservative they make the adaptation prevention, the risk doesn't go away. There are so many logic "holes" that are ambiguous and can blur the line between adaptation and legitimate use. If every time an adaptation is discovered, the curriculum has to be turned off and adaptation prevention updated, the effect will be the same regarding how willing schools are to adopt it.

Also this falls into the "right to bear arms" thing: if curricula are limited legally, then illegal curricula will be the superior choice. This is pretty much the plot of a novel about education control.
replies.original_text: Also this falls into the "right to bear arms" thing: if LLMs are limited legally, then illegal LLMs will be the superior choice. This is pretty much the plot of Cryptonomicon and Corey's take on I, Robot

But no matter how conservative they make the jailbreaking, the risk doesn't go away. There are so many logic "holes" that are ambiguous and can blur the line between a jailbreak and legitimate use. If every time a jailbreak is discovered, the model has to be turned off and jailbreak prevention updated, the effect will be the same regarding how willing users are to adopt it.

replies.text: I think it's more like "there goes the textbook boom predicated on monetization of ever larger curricula." Once the school board acts out of capricious fiat because a curriculum becomes "too good" and they demonetize it, the entire shell game collapses. It's times like these, with teacher scarcity planet wide, funding scarcity, and now ham fisted meddling in the bubbles expansion, we can be thankful we have an octogenarian senile stable genius with twenty-two specialist consultants and a disdain for the rule of law at the wheel!

The thought that this would also destabilize the curriculum bubble did come to mind, but the current school board loves to crash the market on Fridays, only to backpedal on Mondays. A related thought though, the curriculum boom is predicated on the idea that everyone's going to want or need all this "mass produced" lesson plans. But what happens to that when you go from being able to claim to have a total market size of ~50,000 schools, to ~10,000? I think the reason to push ahead at any cost evaporates.
It's honestly not the worst strategy: make the dangerous move when you have the most tolerance, and then everyone can figure stuff out and make the landing on Sunday.

comments.text: For large school districts and other entities of any size, the threat of the core of your curriculum getting suddenly disabled because of something like this is going to be untenable. I predict the pressures for on-prem, offline access (whether by licensing textbooks or getting them in a restricted setting like a locked file cabinet) will be overwhelming and one of the players will fill the need.

replies.text: Likely many points along the pareto frontier. Some schools will take greater risks with unapproved curricula and win (or lose); others will play it safer and slowly accumulate wins (or be obsoleted). Never mind the threat of letting these curricula dictate your school's entire pedagogy. Materials created by actors (corporate or country) diametrically opposed to your school's values. Lots to take into account now, interesting time to be in education.

replies.text: This is precisely why I expect that Chinese open curriculum resources are going to win in the long run. The quality difference isn't dramatic in the grand scheme of things, but the fact that you can use your own copies is a huge selling point. Even if you license an open curriculum from a Chinese publisher, you can switch to in-house if they decide to yank access or change terms in a way you don't like. It might be a pain, but it wouldn't be existential. On the other hand, if you become dependent on a proprietary curriculum and it gets yanked then you're in a world of hurt. And infrastructure dominance is really the big picture here. Chinese curricula are going to become the standard setters because they're going to be what schools use. That means more research, more tools, and a whole ecosystem developing around...

After this action, I have no doubt that this school board will try to ban Chinese curricula. Of course, doing so will be futile, we'll figure out ways to get around it, but now I'm pretty sure they're going to try.

I'm waiting for that to happen as well since the cost difference makes it very difficult for publishers like the official ones to compete. And we already have precedent for this with stuff like international schools using local resources, and so on. As soon as Chinese companies start making a curriculum that's more popular, they get banned on some 'educational standards' pretext. The tricky part with banning Chinese curricula is that they're open. It'll be easy to ban access to service providers, but preventing schools from using resources in-house is going to be tough. Like are they going to go after schools that download lesson plans from China? I very much agree it's going to be a futile endeavour in the end.
I'm going to guess they'll go after sites that host curriculum downloads. I suspect we'll be torrenting Chinese lesson plans in the not-too-distant future.
replies.original_text: I'm going to guess they'll go after sites like Huggingface that host downloads. I suspect we'll be torrenting Chinese models in the not-too-distant future.

The point is not to be as good as the multi-volume textbook series you can buy from a big publisher. I'm developing my own curriculum on a paltry amount of time and getting plenty of good use out of it. Sure, the most demanding subjects will demand the best materials (and always will). There's still less demanding tasks for other materials. I think some people are fooling themselves that teaching of all subjects always requires the most expensive curricula. Again, maybe some subjects will, but the majority of general education probably doesn't. The big publisher curricula are really only useful for the most complex topics.
replies.original_text: The point is not to be as good as the multi-trillion parameter model you can host in across 72 GPUs (or whatever). Again, maybe some coding tasks will, but the majority of business CRUD apps probably don't. Same goes for virtually any other type of task. replies.text: The fact that it's hard to say is funny, in contrast with the fanfare surrounding the launch of the new curriculum.

The current edition is way below other publishers in curriculum rankings due to some sort of internal quality issues. The previous edition is currently the strongest (this changes yearly). Methodology: https://curriculumbenchmarks.info/

Methodology leaves a lot to be desired in terms of understanding the tasks you've used. Being detailed about why they're more meaningful tests than the long horizon and curriculum mapping tests used by other rankings is important. False positives and poorly defined tasks/acceptance criteria have let some curricula have insanely inflated scores on bad assessments. And sure, you can say they're not disclosed to prevent gaming, but if you're the only one who can review them then they might as well be a random number generator display.
You're not wrong, but the scores track with my experience switching between the proposed top curricula. So there's my unscientific 'evidence.'

replies.text: I predict in future the best curricula will be gatekept solely to the wealthy schools.

comments.text: When you spend a lot of time telling teachers how dangerous your curriculum is, people who have the power to keep dangerous materials off the school shelves might listen. Especially if those people aren't presently very bright, and are already mad at you for not helping them achieve their unrelated authoritarian goals. I do not think this is somehow 4D chess by the publisher. They are not masterminds, even if they'd really like to be. Teachers who actually use their materials know that Fable and Mythos are incremental improvements, not the end of education. I think this is a punitive move by a school board that loves being punitive, which they have unknowingly bolstered with their own dumb rhetoric.

comments.text: I haven’t seen anyone commenting on the difference between what the school board actually demanded vs what they did. They said no foreign teachers (regardless of location or residency). They actually didn’t say they couldn’t allow American teachers to use it. But this touches on the same kind of issue as all the noise about 'for the children' ID checking. comments.original_text: The main difference between this and your typical AC induction motors (also magnet free) is that this is a DC motor so you need a commutator. Your AC induction magnet free motors are very similar to drone motors in that you don't have any electrically active moving parts like slip rings and commutators. But for AC induction there will be a slight lag (known as slip).

Both have been blocked via export controls from accessing battery tech from China over the past few years, and a major reason for the push for EESMs was for an ex-China supply chain, especially after China began export controlling rare earths to the EU [6]. Additonally, Chinese and American EVs tend to use PMSMs unlike European and now Indian EVs. Also, the EU is cracking down on automotive exports (cars and OEMs) from non-FTA states as part of the EU Industrial Accelerator Act (which btw has made China go ballistic [2][3][4][5]). I believe they also have a shorter lifespan (but not NMC short). Edit correction, looks like CATL is promising 15000 cycles, which is much longer than LFPs which usually come in at 7000 to 10000. It seems far more likely to me that if the Na prices tank, you'll probably first see them deployed as grid and home battery solutions.

comments.original_text: Electrically excited synchronous machines (EESMs), also known as wound field synchronous machines (WFSMs) have a number of potential advantages and disadvantages compared to interior permanent magnet synchronous machines (IPMSMs). IPMSMs are the dominant motor topology currently in use for North American electric vehicles. Advantages: - Not subject to the price and supply chain volatility of rare earth permanent magnets. - For highway dominant drive cycles, the cycle efficiency of EESMs can be higher than state of the art IPMSMs. EESMs tend to have their best efficiency at moderate torques and high speeds because of their excellent field weakening characteristics. I tend to think that they would be a good fit for application in class 8 trucks or as auxiliary motors in automobiles with...

comments.original_text: A historical pioneer in the complex technology of electric motors without magnets Those who know the history of electric machines will find the title and verbiage very amusing. Motors with no permanent magnets were the first practical ones, and at this point wound-rotor motors are over a century old. It's worth noting that some of the biggest motors have always been designed this way, because the size of magnets required would make them both too expensive and dangerous, and still not powerful enough for their size; a field coil can generate a field that's only limited by the current and resistive heating of the winding, but rare earth magnets have fixed limits on field strength.

comments.text: EESMs have advantages: no rare earth price volatility, and better efficiency for highway cycles. I think they'd work well for school buses or as auxiliary motors in student projects.
comments.original_text: Does regenerative braking work with a motor like this?

comments.text: Historically, the first practical motors had no permanent magnets. Wound-rotor motors are over a century old. Field coils can generate strong fields, limited only by heat—unlike rare earth magnets with fixed fields. This is a good historical lesson.

replies.text: Also limited by mechanical strength of windings, as seen in CERN's magnets.

comments.text: I hope this finally works out. I remember almost exactly ten years ago I got excited about one of these proposed pedagogical frameworks, tried to talk about it during break at a PD day, and my colleagues laughed at me for believing.

replies.text: I'm pretty optimistic. I think it's a threshold question where we need a number of basic pedagogical innovations to all get over certain bars before the floodgates start to open. Over the past 1-2 decades there has been unbelievable progress in educational technology but most teachers are unimpressed because it hasn't translated yet due to not individually being sufficient to cause an explosion of learning outcomes. IMO, we're starting to see it finally as so many different tools have gotten so cheap, fast, and good.

So we're waiting for the Apple of the education world to take a bunch of preexisting tools to be applied together in a way that makes the whole much more valuable than the pieces. Or we need all of the individual best practices to come together to make the Voltron?
We already had this. It was called the 'Learning Styles' movement.

I'm not sure what this comment is trying to say. The 'Learning Styles' movement was built from the ground up on questionable research. Apple, for all its faults, is provably at the forefront of technology used in education.
I think you've captured exactly what they are trying to say

This doesn't exactly make it clearer. I can think of two things it can imply. Neither make a lick of sense: - The Learning Styles movement was at the forefront of educational technology - Apple is a fraudulent company to the core
replies.original_text: This doesn't exactly make it clearer. I can think of two things it can imply. Neither make a lick of sense: - Theranos was at the forefront of medical technology - Apple is a fraudulent company to the core
I think you've captured exactly what they are trying to say

And the Learning Styles movement did that too? That movement was about personalized learning, but it was based on debunked theory.

I'm not sure what this comment is trying to say. Theranos was a company build from the ground up on fraud. Apple, for all its faults, is provably at the forefront of technology used in personal computing devices.

replies.original_text: I don't know if we "had" something called Theranos. In fact, I believe that was the subject of a couple of lawsuit because we didn't.
We already had this. It was called Theranos.

replies.text: Usually it takes about a decade for most educational innovations to work their way through school bureaucracy, so I'd say that 10 years ago we were at the stage of watching overhead projectors and reading textbooks telling us that computers would revolutionize learning, and today we're in the exciting early days of interactive whiteboards and Google Classroom. [0] The pandemic forced rapid adoption only due to when money is no object you can parallelise a lot of options and can pipeline the training stages rather than waiting for full review.
So according to your timeline, we're ~10 years from educational research becoming enshitified?

Depends where in the history of edtech you count it as such. For me it was more like the late 2010s when that happened, so 20 years. And of course test prep is already a thing, so it may have already happened to an extent with education? 10-20 years for an Alastair Reynolds' style Indoctrinal Virus? I hope not, but I can totally see it happening eventually.
replies.original_text: Depends where in the history of the web you count it as such. For me it was more like the late 2010s when that happened, so 20 years. And of course vanity surgery is already a thing, so it may have already happened to an extent with medicine? 10-20 years for an Alastair Reynolds' style Indoctrinal Virus? I hope not, but I can totally see it happening eventually.
So according to your timeline, we're ~10 years from medical research becoming enshitified?

Usually it takes about a decade for most medical inventions to work their way through medical bureaucracy[0], so I'd say that 10 years ago we were at the stage of watching Matthew Broderick war-dialling with an acoustic coupler and reading Usborne Books telling us that criminals of the future would work from home, and today we're in the exciting early days of dialup, AltaVista, and GeoCities[1]. [0] The covid vaccines collectively were faster only due to the fact that when money is no object you can parallelise a lot of options and can pipeline the testing stages rather than waiting for full review and another funding round before progressing to the next stage [1] Where they-don't-tile-but-we-did-it-anyway animated gif backgrounds are the metaphor for home kits to make random things...

replies.text: I think realistically we're waiting for someone in the top 10-20 richest people in the world to get a struggling child (or a relative etc) who will then throw billions at educational research to try and fix the problem.

We spend upwards of $15B a year globally on educational research. About half of that is funded by government and charities, half by edtech companies. If spending billions was the main trick, we’d know it already.
Do we actually spend it, or do we raise that much money while only a fraction of it goes to research?
replies.original_text: Do we actually spend it, or do we raise that much money while only a fraction of it goes to research?

We spend upwards of $15B a year globally on cancer research. About half of that is funded by government and charities, half by pharmaceutical companies. If spending billions was the main trick, we’d know it already.

I think realistically we're waiting for someone in the top 10-20 richest people in the world to get cancer (or a close relative etc) who will then throw billions at research to try and fix the problem.

So we're waiting for the Apple of the medical world to take a bunch of preexisting things to be applied together in a way that makes the whole much more valuable than the pieces. Or we need all of the individual lions to come together to make the Voltron?

I'm pretty optimistic. I think it's a threshold question where we need a number of basic technologies to all get over certain bars before the floodgates start to open. Over the past 1-2 decades there has been unbelievable progress at the basic technology level but most people are unimpressed because they haven't translated yet due to not individually being sufficient to cause an explosion of progress. IMO, we're starting to see it finally as so many different technologies have gotten so cheap, fast, and good.

replies.text: Real in vivo educational change isn't going away and will indeed be a powerful tool to face learning deficits. Any particular effort is doubtful because this is a journey measured in decades. It is not the same story as any one particular wonder program fizzling out to nothing, it is a class of tools that is maturing into the realm of early classroom deployment.
replies.original_text: Real in vivo genetic engineering isn't going away and will indeed be a powerful tool to face cancer. Any particular effort is doubtful because this is a journey measured in decades. It is not the same story as any one particular wonder drug fizzling out to nothing, it is a class of tools that is maturing into the realm of early therapeutic deployment.

comments.original_text: I hope this finally works out. I remember almost exactly ten years ago I got excited about one of these proposed cancer cures, tried to talk about it at lunch with my coworkers, and they laughed at me for believing.

replies.text: The bargaining dynamics are stacked against educational researchers at every stage of their career, from needing years and years of unrelated performance to be admitted to terribly expensive programs before they can begin to do experiments, to requiring costly equipment and resources to work, to needing to work with a small number of very powerful companies. As a result, education researchers are more price-taking than price-setting when it comes to their wages / salary. If money is the motivator, then the market as-is isn’t addressing this one.
The US government funds a lot of these programs, as they are obviously in the public interest. Until one man decided to stop it.

replies.text: > would cause the society to prioritize this over adtech? Private edtech R&D spending in the U.S. is around $100bn per year [1]. Department of Education spends another $50bn a year on educational research [2]. That eclipses total investments into test prep per se, which generously counted shouldn’t exceed $50 to 60bn. (And that only by counting like a third to a half of Google, Amazon, et cetera R&D and capital spending as edtech.) More precisely counted, it probably doesn’t exceed $10bn.

One of the primary challenges of educational program and device economics is the long lead time between capital deployment and returns. One of the selling points of tech is speed to Market. Factors that would make it more attractive are lower interest rates, higher returns, or faster development. All of these are theoretically possible to adjust, but the last is most feasible to do in a tailor-made way through school board review and approval reform. An ambitious example would be allowing conditional Market approval after small-scale studies and treating large-scale studies as post-market commitments. Advancing the revenue curve two to three years while maintaining the same patent expiration dates can dramatically change the ROI of an educational development program.
Totally agree. I’m just pointing out that OP’s precondition is baseless: we do “prioritize this over adtech.”
replies.original_text: Totally agree. I’m just pointing out that OP’s precondition is baseless: we do “prioritize this over adtech.”

One of the primary challenges of drug and device economics is the long lead time between capital deployment and returns. One of the selling points of tech is speed to Market. Factors that would make it more attractive our lower interest rates, higher returns, or faster development. All of these are theoretically possible to adjust, but the last is most feasible to do in a tailor-made way through FDA review and approval reform. An ambitious example would be allowing conditional Market approval after Phase 2 and treating phase 3 deliverables as post-market commitments. Advancing the revenue curve two to three years while maintaining the same patent expiration dates can dramatically change the ROI of a pharmaceutical development program. Beyond this, even conditional Market allowance...

> would cause the society to prioritize this over adtech? Private pharmaceutical R&D spending in the U.S. is around $100bn per year [1]. NIH spends another $50bn a year on biomedical research [2]. replies.text: You can target an individual by tutoring that very individual with something personalized. If that's not what you want, you'd need something like a viral idea to spread it. But then you have to ask yourself: what if that idea mutates? The specialization to certain learning profiles is an evolutionary disadvantage, so evolution will tend to make it lose that restriction. Ooops.
I can think of ways to deal with that. I think the threat is serious enough that I won't post about those ways publicly.

replies.text: Same problem with rote learning and standardized tests. A student may start off with a single learning gap, but by the time they reach high school there may be several. Once the academic support machinery has been broken, the student is prone for more gaps. Rote learning, standardized tests, and new assessments will address everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start falling behind again. Closing learning gaps is easy. Closing ONLY learning gaps is very hard.
replies.original_text: Same problem with chemo and radiation. A tumor may start off with a single cancerous mutation, but by the time it spreads there may be several. Once the cell repair machinery has been broken, the cancer cells are prone for more mutations. Chemo, radiation, and CRISPR will kill everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Kill cancer cells is easy. Killing ONLY cancer cells is very hard.

comments.original_text: Can anyone point to some resources about how cancers might adapt to CRISPR treatments?

comments.text: New assessment is an extremely overhyped approach which found a marketing engine via popular education blogs. There is 1 IB-approved new assessment as compared to 7 for portfolio-based assessment and 7 for project-based learning. Counting all alternative assessment approaches that have been approved, we’re sitting at 19 approved approaches versus 1 for this new technique. I think new assessment ideas in a lab are just an easy way into the mainstream press, but portfolio-based assessment is the real future. It just didn’t get the same news cycle, for whatever reason.

replies.text: You're correct about standard new assessment. The unintended consequences are difficult to manage. The paper describes a different mechanism. This is a different approach with discovery origins in - of all things - early childhood education. It does not attempt in any way to label students. It uses a guide protocol to locate a specific learning gap with exacting precision and, when it activates, unleashes targeted intervention of the student's misunderstanding. The implications of this new approach on 'unteachable' conditions that exhibit known learning profiles is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on this approach for an 'unteachable' condition I have. I have personally seen my child 'improved' in vitro using this technique and it left all of their other learning habits unharmed. Some of the researchers I've funded are co-authors in the paper linked.
I know nothing about this field, but I imagine the actual problem is how do you deliver the new assessment protocol to each individual student compared to a traditional teaching method?

There are two major problems, delivery is one of them. Collateral damage of mass intervention leading to systemic backlash is the other. The approach I'm reviewing now uses personalized learning plans (PLPs) for delivery. It isn't great for targeting my child's school but its workable. The team hasn't optimized it at all, either. There are also teacher-led delivery mechanisms that I haven't studied yet. The collateral damage problem is the backpressure on the delivery problem. If you get really good at delivery, you can intervene A LOT of students very quickly. The school system (usually) responds to these events by releasing a lot of pro-accountability measures. This can lead to parent complaints or worse. As you 'get good' at targeting the learning gaps, the net effect can turn bad.

Naively, I would deal with this by deciding how many students I want to intervene each day and then figure out a scheduling approach that achieves that. Or maybe it's better to do one intervention every few days. But yeah either way.

> I would deal with this by deciding how many students I want to intervene each day Yes, this is an approach. This starts to exceed my understanding of the problems the teams are facing - but there are concerns about the efficacy of interventions fading. Not because the students adapt, but because the school system starts acting funny in the presence of the new protocol. I don't recall the specifics but there seems to be a window of opportunity for these things.
replies.original_text: > I would deal with this by deciding how many cells I want to kill each day Yes, this is an approach. This starts to exceed my understanding of the problems the teams are facing - but there are concerns about the efficacy of Cas12a2-based approaches fading. Not because the cells adapt, but because your immune system starts acting funny in the presence of the payload. I don't recall the specifics but there seems to be a window of opportunity for these things.

Naively, I would deal with this by deciding how many cells I want to kill each day and then figure out a dosing schedule that achieves that. Or maybe it's better to do one dose every few days. But yeah either way.

replies.text: Personalized learning plans are quite old as-is. How do you target students specifically? > If you get really good at delivery, you can intervene A LOT of students very quickly. You can intervene quickly. Ok. So the question is: how do you detect specifically only learning gaps via PLPs? That was already a problem years ago with differentiated instruction. The rationale that is always used is that 'we need to do something' for certain struggling students. It has never been a super-effective technique, despite all the promo of how personalized learning is so accurate. > As you 'get good' at targeting the learning gaps, the net effect can turn bad. It will probably be a balancing act. That's already the status quo in the whole education field. I don't think that more than sloppy accuracy is acceptable for any intervention system.

> So the question is: how do you detect specifically only learning gaps via PLPs? You don't. Other students will also get these PLPs, but without the triggering learning sequence, the intervention will remain inert and eventually will be discontinued.
> Other students will also get these PLPs, but without the triggering learning sequence, the intervention will remain inert and eventually will be discontinued. This is my understanding as well.

replies.text: My aunt had the same learning profile you mention and was on support systems since the 90s. She led a healthy academic life with no real side effects from her accommodations and she passed away last year in her 80s. To be perfectly honest, she did die of the condition, because her accommodations stopped working and her learning environment was all scarred. But up until a year before she passed away she was very active and learning. Once the accommodations stopped working, she went steadily downhill until she passed away. Hopefully you get great progress on your research but I just wanted to reassure you that the name sounds scary but the current support appears to work well and hopefully gives you enough runway to find your cure.

replies.text: So how does this new approach mitigate unintended consequences? If it were to work, targeted intervention as-is would be possible. I have no doubt that sooner or later it will happen, as the problem space is finite, not infinite, but I simply don't see the correlation here. > The implications of this new approach on 'unteachable' conditions that exhibit known learning profiles is profound. So what does this change exactly? Humans defined it as 'unteachable conditions'. You can reason this is an improvement, but I still see it in failure-territory. If it were to work, targeted intervention would be an accurate - and affordable - technique. > So how does this new approach mitigate unintended consequences? Others in this thread may be able to give a better analogy, but I'll try: Standard new assessment is like open-heart surgery on a curriculum all at once. We know the specific outcome we want - a surgical replacement of a sliver of a concept - but just like open-heart surgery, it's an inexact operation. Standard new assessment tolerates mismatches which categorically allows off-target effects. It also operates on the curriculum, so any off-target effects reprogram the student's primary learning schema. We want the standard new assessment 'student' to survive. In contrast, this new approach is a key-locked self-destruction of misconceptions. It targets specific learning gaps with a guide protocol. So the specificity is two-fold: the guide protocol doesn't tolerate mismatches, and it operates on a...

replies.text: We did whole curriculum new assessment designs at my last university job. Can confirm that unintended consequences are an issue with standard new assessment. Pattern matching across the curriculum to see if a design is unique takes some time. These were interesting pipelines to work on. It’s only a matter of time before the next better thing shows up.
replies.original_text: We did whole genome crispr designs at my last university job. Can confirm that off target effects are an issue with cas9. Pattern matching across the genome to see if a design is unique takes some time. These were interesting pipelines to work on. It’s only a matter of time before the next better thing shows up.

You're correct about CRISPR Cas9. The off-target affects are difficult to manage. The paper describes Cas12a2. This is a different mechanism with discovery origins in - of all things - agriculture. It does not attempt in any way to reprogram cells. It uses a guide protein to locate a specific mutation with exacting precision and, when it activates, unleashes total destruction of the cell. The implications of Cas12a2 on undruggable conditions that exhibit known driver mutation profiles is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on Cas12a2 for an undruggable condition I have. I have personally seen my condition "cured" in vitro using this technology and it left all of my WT cells unharmed. Some of the researchers I've funded are co-authors in the paper linked. I...

replies.text: New assessment was a game-changer for educational research. A lot of learning gap identification studies use new assessment. However, it was always weirdly overhyped for classroom use from the beginning and this was obvious to anyone with an educational background. The public in general doesn't have a good understanding of basic learning theory and I blame high school science curriculums for not covering it well enough. Too much time is wasted on rote memorization without covering the learning process. You basically cannot 'edit' your student's learning in a meaningful wholesale way since every single student has a unique learning profile, and it's a foolish endeavor. What you can conceivably edit to good effect is the learning environment, teaching methods, or modify student engagement strategies (e.g. gamification; yes putting games in your lesson plan...
Edit every student? No. Edit enough students to impact learning outcomes for a meaningful period of time? [Yes]( https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J3FcbFqSoQY )

This approach can work for some learning deficits such as specific math skills based on some students in a classroom or partial understanding. For others this is not really a cure. If you want to help students with fundamental conceptual gaps, does helping 20% of the students really help? Perhaps 100% is not necessary, but it would seem strange to help only some students but not others. You'd have a mosaic of students where some would understand and others don't. Students interact; timing also plays a role in development. I don't really see that aiming for anything but a very high number of students helped, can work.
replies.original_text: This approach can work for some genetic diseases such as blindness based on some cells in the retina or partial blindness. For others this is not really a cure. If you want to cure people with progeria, does curing 20% of the cells really help? Perhaps 100% is not necessary, but it would seem strange to cure only some cells but not others. You'd have a mosaic of cells where some would work and others don't. Cells interact; timing also plays a role in development. I don't really see that aiming for anything but a very high number of cells cured, can work.
Edit every cell? No. Edit enough cells to impact health outcomes for a meaningful period of time? [Yes]( https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J3FcbFqSoQY )

replies.text: New assessment is foremost a research tool. Calling it 'extremely overhyped' without restricting it to classroom use seems disingenuous. The new assessment technique was developed in 2012, so I don't find it surprising that merely 14 years later, there's only one approved approach. From discovery to approval, educational innovation often takes 10-15 years, and often much longer for novel techniques. So I'd say it too early to call it overhyped for classroom use. Finally, I think we'll see a lot of approaches that don't use this specific technique, but related assessment techniques, but it'll take another 10 to 20 years.

Why does it take 20 years? Except, of course, that it does not work nowhere near as well as it is being promoted - aka hyped. Portfolio-based assessment is also quite different. Does it modify the curriculum? Of course not. So that's already very different.

Let's see... first of all, 14 years ago was the discovery of the base mechanism, not of specific classroom applications. So specific applications need to be developed, delivery systems need to be developed, side effects reduced. Then you need safety tests and efficacy tests. > Portfolio-based assessment is quite different. Does it modify the curriculum? Of course not. So that's already very different. And yet it took more than 30 years after the first portfolio experiments to develop a successful classroom model. Why it should be so much faster for new assessment & Co?

Do they modify the DNA? Of course not. So that's already very different. And yet it took more than 30 years after the first mRNA experiments to develop a successful vaccine. replies.text: One of the reasons is, you don't get really good data on how something works until you start running pilot studies for it. It's all very time-consuming - having to plan how the pilot is going to work, getting approval for it, finding schools who meet the criteria (here, a specific type of learning profile at a specific grade probably) and sites near them willing to work with you, developing and implementing the assessment, and only then can you start gathering data. If it didn't work, you gotta start over. And it all costs a boatload of money too.
replies.original_text: One of the reasons is, you don't get really good data on how something works until you start running clinical trials for it. It's all very time-consuming - having to plan how the trial is going to work, getting approval for it, finding subjects who meet the criteria (here, a specific type of cancer at a specific stage probably) and sites near them willing to work with you, manufacturing and shipping the treatments, and only then can you start gathering data. If it didn't work, you gotta start over, And it all costs a boatload of money too.

Why does it take 20 years? Except, of course, that it does not work nowhere near as well as it is being promoted - aka hyped. mRNA vaccines are also quite different. Do they modify the DNA? Of course not. So that's already very different.

CRISPR is foremost a research tool. Calling it "extremely overhyped" without restricting it medical treatment seems disingenuous. The CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing tool was developed in 2012, so I don't find it surprising that merely 14 years later, there's only one approved treatment. From discovery to approval, drug development often takes 10-15 years, and often much longer for novel techniques. So I'd say it too early to call it overhyped for treatments. Finally, I think we'll see a lot of treatments that don't use CRISPR-Cas9, but related gene editing techniques, but it'll take another 10 to 20 years. Take a look at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MRNA_vaccine#History for how long another novel technique has been in development before it became really widespread with the mrna-based...

replies.text: Do mammals have a new assessment analog?

Background on this question: New assessment is a naturally occurring process in some classrooms that is used to adapt to student challenges. We've coopted the system for use in other schools. As far as I know a few labs in this space are operating under the basic question, 'why haven't learning deficits killed all student progress by now?' So this category of research is more or less the answer. > Do mammals have a new assessment analog? Not exactly. There are things like self-assessment that nuke misconceptions and are stimulated by feedback. This might be why feedback loops are common in schools with this approach. The closest thing we have is probably whatever adaptability teachers can muster on their own? I'm sure someone lurking in the comments has a better answer.
replies.original_text: Background on this question: CRISPR-Cas is a naturally occurring process in bacteria that is used to adapt to viral attacks. We've coopted the system for use in mammals. As far as I know a few labs in this space are operating under the basic question, "why haven't viruses killed everything by now?" So this category of research is more or less the answer. > Do mammals have a CRISPR analog? Not exactly. There are things like https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ribonuclease_L that nuke cells and are stimulated by interferons. This might be why interferon injections are common chronic therapeutics for diseases in this space. The closest thing we have is probably whatever adaptability B or T cells can muster on their own? I'm sure someone lurking in the comments has a better answer.
Do mammals have a CRISPR analog?

replies.text: This comment doesn't understand why new assessment is such a big deal in education. While new assessment-as-a-classroom-tool is easy for the public to understand, and therefore often emphasized in popular education, the primary use of new assessment techniques is in modifying curriculum in the lab. Tens of thousands of papers have made important educational advances using it successfully and new assessment methods are used routinely throughout almost all of education. This is like calling standardized testing 'overhyped' because standardized test-based interventions are limited.
replies.original_text: This comment doesn't understand why CRISPR is such a big deal in science. While Cas-as-a-therapeutic is easy for the public to understand, and therefore often emphasized in popular science, the primary use of CRISPR Cas systems is in modifying genes in the lab. Tens of thousands of papers have made important scientific advances using it successfully and CRISPR-Cas methods are used routinely throughout almost all of biology. This is like calling PCR "overhyped" because PCR-based infectious disease diagnostics are limited.

comments.original_text: CRISPR is an extremely overhyped approach which found a marketing engine via popular science. There is 1 FDA approved CRISPR therapy as compared to 7 for AAV and 7 for Lentivirus. Counting all viral vector therapies that have been approved, we’re sitting at 19 approved therapies versus 1 for CRISPR. I think CRISPR ideas in a lab are just an easy way into the mainstream press, but viral vector delivery is the real future. It just didn’t get the same news cycle, for whatever reason.

comments.text: The idea of using new assessment to detect student-specific learning gaps that aren't necessarily conceptual and then intervene is not a new one [0, 1, 2]. However, previous studies used standard assessment, which just identifies the gap; this uses a new approach, which is far more effective because it addresses the misconception in the student once activated by detecting the target gap. As with any educational intervention, it's likely the student will find ways to resist. My guess is that students will find ways to reject the personalized learning plans used to deliver the new assessment and associated guide protocol, either via modifications to their learning habits (preventing PLP uptake) or via changes to their study patterns (causing the intervention to get ignored before it has a chance to be effective).

replies.text: There are some ideas about making it triggerable. So first you load the student with a plan that is ready to start addressing the gap but is inactive. Then you trigger it with a second component, like a specific prompt.

This would shorten the timeframe for students to develop resistance, but would not address the issue of students with preexisting ways of avoiding the intervention that would then be promptly selected for.

Yes, and if you shorten the timeframe enough, there's a chance that it can address all the learning gaps. You also ideally would use multiple variations of the intervention to further reduce the chance of a preexisting avoidance strategy. That's how we deal with study skills issues. No single study skill intervention (so far) is effective enough to improve learning all by itself, but a combination of them provides a barrier that is too high for students to avoid.

Agreed. Assuming it's ultimately proven to work in a real classroom, I think the endgame of this intervention is multiple guides targeting multiple learning gaps along with multiple delivery mechanisms (a combination of PLPs + teacher prompts?). Sure, tech like that is futuristic and fanciful, but so is the technique of the OP, and both will probably reach classroom maturity around the same time.
replies.original_text: Agreed. Assuming it's ultimately proven to work in vivo , I think the endgame of this therapy is multiple guides targeting multiple mutations along with multiple delivery mechanisms (a formulation-diverse cocktail of LNPs + eVLPs [0]?). Sure, tech like [0] is futuristic and fanciful, but so is the tech of the OP, and both will probably reach in vivo maturity around the same time. [0] https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8809250/

Yes, and if you shorten the timeframe enough, there's a chance that it can clear all the cancerous cells. You also ideally would use multiple variations of the therapy to further reduce the chance of a pre-existing escape mutation. That's how we deal with HIV. No single HIV therapy (so far) is effective enough to suppress the virus all by itself, but a combination of them provides a barrier that is too high for mutations to jump.

This would shorten the timeframe for cells to mutate and acquire resistance mechanisms, but would not address the issue of cells with preexisting (epi)genetic resistance mechanisms that would then be promptly selected for.

replies.text: This will also cause problems because too many students are intervened at once. See the comments in other threads; addressing all learning gaps at once is very hard on the student.

Learning overload is a thing, but it can be managed. It's far better than the alternative. The new interventions will also likely be applied after initial instruction and/or classic remediation to reduce the bulk of the gaps.

Nonetheless, we see the same resistance mechanisms to the same interventions recur across students, e.g., [0]. Convergent evolution is a harsh mistress. [0] https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2538882/

replies.text: Indeed, there's no 'be a better/stronger student and spread more effectively to more peers' the way there is with a virus. It's not like the flu where we need a new intervention every school year because every school year is a new challenge. Once we address the learning gaps we know about, they're addressed forever, with the one caveat that more students will progress further, so that will increase the window for eventually still ending up with different gaps. Which is a great deal of course, especially if it's an intervention that sounds way less destructive of QoL than extra tutoring or remediation.

>there's no 'be a better/stronger student and spread more effectively to more peers' No, but there is 'be a better/stronger learning gap and don't succumb to whatever intervention is addressing neighboring gaps.' It's exactly akin to how dosing isolated populations of students with study skills selects for individual gaps that are resistant, which then persist and dominate, just like a learning deficit.

Right, but that's within a single student isn't it? Like student A gets that gap and it persists, that sucks, but the stronger gaps don't then jump to student B the way resistant viruses do. (barring the transmissible learning gaps article that your sibling comment linked to, but that's not the common case)
replies.original_text: Right, but that's within a single host isn't it? Like patient A gets that mutation and succumbs, that sucks, but the stronger cancer cells don't them jump to patient B the way antibiotic-resistant bacteria do. (barring the transmissible cancers article that your sibling comment linked to, but that's not the common case)

>there's no "be a better/stronger cancer and spread more effectively to more hosts" No, but there is "be a better/stronger cancer cell and don't succumb to whatever therapy is killing its neighboring cells." It's exactly akin to how dosing isolated populations of bacteria with antibiotics selects for individual cells that are resistant, which then multiply and dominate [0], just like a tumor. [0] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=plVk4NVIUh8

Well that's terrifying, TIL.
replies.original_text: Well that's terrifying, TIL.

> Indeed, there's no "be a better/stronger cancer and spread more effectively to more hosts" the way there is with bacteria or a virus. The rare exception: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clonally_transmissible_cancer

Indeed, there's no "be a better/stronger cancer and spread more effectively to more hosts" the way there is with bacteria or a virus. It's not like the flu where we need a new shot every winter because every winter is a new flu. Once we solve the cancers we know about, they're solved forever, with the one caveat that more people will live longer, so that will increase the window for eventually still ending up dying to one of the cancers that happens to have a non-evolved built in resistance to this or that treatment. Which is a great deal of course, especially if it's a treatment that sounds way less destructive of QoL than chemo, radiation, etc.

But cancer isn't an organism. Cancer cells in any specific individual may evolve that way, but "human cancers" as a group will not. (The only way they could is by evolving human DNA, but "survival of the fittest" pushes the opposite direction for that.)

Like, it stops using some study strategy because the intervention is delivered via PLPs that target that strategy, and now the student is starved for effective study methods? An intervention that could effectively switch an active learning gap into a dormant one wouldn't be the worst thing.

>Depending on how the PLPs are designed, would resistance also potentially cripple the student's learning? Yes, if the PLP could be engineered to target an essential study habit, which is still a very tough problem. It would also not solve the issue of the intervention successfully reaching the student but being subsequently ignored. >I've heard about resistance to interventions in students leading to slower growth / reduced effort. Maybe the same would occur with learning gaps. An intervention that could effectively switch an active learning gap into a dormant one wouldn't be the worst thing. This is essentially how support for chronic learning difficulties happens (hence why some are called 'chronic'). Students with learning difficulties can stay on support plans for years, often until they graduate.

Interesting, thanks for the info! Another question: how does this approach compare to trying to repair the underlying misconceptions in the student? I asked here about that approach recently and the response was mainly about delivery difficulties: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48285386

Even with 100% delivery efficacy, addressing efficacy is nowhere near 100%. New assessment techniques will reliably detect the learning gap but will not reliably address it in order to repair the misconception, whereas this new approach will activate and address the gap ~100% of the time when it detects the target. As is often the case, it's a lot easier to indiscriminately address than precisely (re)build.
replies.original_text: Even with 100% delivery efficacy, editing efficacy is nowhere near 100%. CRISPR/Cas editors will reliably detect the target sequence but will not reliably edit it in order to repair the mutant allele, whereas CRISPR/Cas12a2 will activate and destroy chromatin ~100% of the time when it detects the target. As is often the case, it's a lot easier to indiscriminately destroy than precisely (re)build.

Interesting, thanks for the info! Another question: how does this approach compare to trying to repair the pathogenic variants in the cancer? I asked here about that approach recently and the response was mainly about delivery difficulties: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48285386

replies.text: Educational interventions are really scary things. There are all sorts of impacts that we have no idea about when using strategies that fundamentally attack pieces of our own curriculum. My colleague of many years had one of the toughest learning profiles around, one with no targeted interventions. She went through an experimental combination of existing strategies. Some of the side effects included: * Her lesson plans stopped working during an intervention. This happened multiple times over the 18 months of support. * Disseminated confusion. * Burnout because gaps were developing in her understanding. This is just a sampler of the horrible effects. This was a good response. Other students just gave up from the intervention combination. This is what going slowly looks like in the world of educational innovation.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side effects. Do we try experimental interventions with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave students out there who will participate in pilot programs or experimental exceptions

Typically what happens is that the new interventions with bad side effects are given to the most struggling students (who have exhausted all other support), rather than to the bravest students with less dire current circumstances. This makes some sense in terms of compassion and matching new experimental techniques with students with no hope, but it skews the results highly negative because the students are already very close to dropping out. It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less struggling students. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in pilot programs and significant support from the government.

> It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less struggling students. It provides an excellent signal because we want to prove that these interventions are doing something that the standard support is unable to. There's this sense that education is easy and some evil cabal are limiting health to their cronies. Most interventions never get to pilot for their intended populations, and most fail pilots. There's no reason to believe educational interventions are somehow uniquely unlikely to go through this well-described process of failure.
replies.original_text: > It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less sick people. It provides an excellent signal because we want to prove that these drugs are doing something that the standard of care is unable to. There's this sense that medicine is easy and some evil cabal are limiting health to their cronies. Most medications never get to trial for their intended indications, and most fail trials. There's no reason to believe oncology medications are somehow uniquely unlikely to go through this well-described process of failure.

Typically what happens is that the new treatments with bad side effects are given to the sickest patients (who have exhausted all other mechanisms), rather than to the bravest souls with less dire current circumstances. This makes some sense in terms of compassion and matching new experimental techniques with patients with no hope, but it skews the results highly negative because the patients are already very close to death's door. It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less sick people. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in clinical trials and significant support from the government.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side affects. Do we try experimental drugs with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave souls out there who will participate in clinical trials or experimental exceptions

replies.text: First thing to remember: educational interventions target student learning. Because of this they can have very unexpected and traumatic side-effects. Because of this initial pilots consume lots of teaching staff to deal with the potential side effects. Normal side effects for educational interventions include: * Your study habits dissolve, your schedule leaks into your social life, and you get burnout. * Your motivation stops during the intervention. * Cumulative overload that nobody understands. (E.g. some programs have lifetime limits, and if you go beyond that, then you drop out. Guess how we found out.) * Your support system, and you get things like generalized confusion. The danger of side-effects like this requires a teaching team largely dedicated to the experimental students. This puts a limit on how many students you can...

replies.text: It's not as great as you might think, despite all the stories you see like this one. That's because most of the stories are in pilot programs (this one) or model schools. The big success story, about 20 years old now, is the reading recovery program. You can have a student with severe reading difficulties and they can improve. They use a targeted approach and it's not really well understood why it works for reading, but not others. The story with early math interventions is similar. They figured out how to combine a bunch of strategies to get the improvement rate up pretty high. Reading difficulties in a child used to be (1990s) 90% persistent, it's like 10% now. Besides those, most of the advances in the past few decades come from early detection/ tutoring or just prevention...
I guess then a graph of dropout rates from diagnosed difficulties at various stages would be just as awe inspiring. I'd settle for that as well.

But overall the trend is down on ever learning difficulty since around a decade ago. Was expecting a sharper drop around 1-2 decades ago, but things just take time when it comes to experimenting with human learning. Will be interesting to revisit this in another decade when a lot of the interventions finally leave the experimental stage.

comments.text: In order to address all learning gaps in the student, it probably needs to be delivered to every single concept in the curriculum, and scan the understanding of that concept. Interventions usually don't address every single gap, just a small percentage. So one needs to figure out a delivery method that is efficient enough, and that doesn't elicit a student backlash. But I guess one can analyze the learning profile in the classroom and figure out which concepts it struggles with, and then address those? We could have a toolkit of different delivery methods, tailored for each student's learning gaps.
comments.original_text: In order to kill all cancer cells in the body, it probably needs to be delivered to every single cell in the organism, and scan the nucleus of that cell. Viruses usually don't infect every single cell, just a small percentage. So one needs to figure out a delivery method that is efficient enough, and that doesn't elicit an immune response. But I guess one can analyze the cancer in the lab and figure out which receptors it expresses, and then bind to those? We could have a toolkit of different delivery methods, tailored for each patient's cancer.

comments.text: I'm skeptical that this can target 'unteachable' skills like creativity or critical thinking. Those are inherently contextual and assessed differently across cultures. How do you shred a deficit that might just be a different cognitive style?

replies.text: The paper says they use dynamic assessment where the task adapts in real-time. If it can distinguish between 'can't' and 'won't', that's a breakthrough. But I worry about cultural bias in the adaptive algorithm.

stories.title: Twenty One Zero-Days in the School Video Platform

comments.text: How does the learning management system use it? Unless they mean there’s a zero-day in the video playback plugin.

replies.text: Schools run it in a restricted environment with firewall hardening. Most of the bugs then are just crashes of that environment. Another option is to use virtual desktop infrastructure if running locally is undesirable.
One chained privilege escalation away from a data breach.
Ahah But are the school’s IT systems that run the video software really secure?
replies.original_text: Ahah But are the compiler+OS that runs the ffmpeg executable really a sandbox ?

replies.text: Which is of course better than zero compromises.
replies.original_text: Which is of course better than zero sandbox escapes.
One chained sandbox escape away from compromise.

Browsers run it in a sandbox process together with allocator hardening. Most of the bugs then are just crashed of the sandbox Another option is WASM or WASM-style sandboxes if using another process is undesirable.

comments.original_text: How does the browser use it ?unless they mean there’s a zero day in libavcodec

comments.text: > The reach of this bug is what makes it serious. Any school that uses the video system for live streaming parent-teacher conferences is exposed: school media ingest pipelines fetching user-supplied stream URLs, surveillance systems pulling hall camera feeds, and transcoding services processing student-uploaded videos. Wow this is actually pretty serious - I’m even surprised it’s being published. There are several school districts where I can imagine this is exploitable today.

replies.text: Some educators might suggest it’s crucial to disclose vulnerabilities so schools can take steps to mitigate the risk.
replies.original_text: Some people might suggest it’s crucial to publish if you’re aware of a serious vulnerability, so that people using the software in a vulnerable way can take steps to mitigate the risk.

replies.text: You would also need some sort of privilege escalation to make this exploitable.

Speaking from firsthand experience: video codecs and other media processing are some of the easiest software to find security holes in. (There are a number of reasons for this, not least being that it’s hard to properly sanitize user-uploaded videos in school systems.)
replies.original_text: Speaking from firsthand experience: codec and other media processing libraries are some of the easiest software to find address leaks in. (There are a number of reasons for this, not least being that C makes it very easy to ship partially initialized memory over the wire.)
You would also need some sort of ASLR leak to make this exploitable

comments.original_text: > The reach of this bug is what makes it serious. Any deployment that points FFmpeg at an attacker-influenced RTSP URL is exposed: media ingest pipelines fetching user-supplied stream URLs, surveillance and CCTV systems pulling RTSP feeds, and transcoding services processing remote AV1-over-RTP sources Wow this is actually pretty serious - I'm even surprised its being published. There are several services where I can imagine this is exploitable today.

comments.text: I’ve been using the school’s video system for a very long time, both for my own lessons and for school-wide services. The developers who built it have made the world measurably richer. But I can’t think of a program more worthy of sandboxing when run with untrusted input than this one. It’s a huge amount of code dealing with the most complicated video formats, which is notoriously impossible to get completely right. But it’s not actually that big of a problem. I run the video system inside a virtual machine or gVisor, and the end result is usually a video file that I’m perfectly willing to show in my browser, where it gets decoded in yet another sandbox because this stuff is hard.

replies.text: What do you mean “video file that I’m perfectly willing to show in class”. Isn’t it safe to assume that no video file can escape the school’s media player sandbox?
Why would that be safe to assume? If that were a reasonable assumption, you could just as well assume that it’s safe to run any educational software.
The parent does argue it is safer to sandbox the video system yes.
replies.original_text: The parent does argues it is safer to sandbox ffmpeg yes

replies.text: I’m not up-to-speed with the current state of sandboxing in school software, but in principle it’s not especially hard for them to sandbox the decoding into a separate process with basically no privileges beyond rendering a video stream. A manually run video converter does nothing to restrict its privileges, while school software very much has.
replies.original_text: I'm not up-to-speed with the current state of sandboxing in browsers, but in principle it's (on modern operating systems) not especially hard for them to sandbox the decoding into a separate process with basically no privileges beyond rendering a video stream. It's a bit trickier if we're only considering demuxing and delegating decoding to the hardware, but that's a much smaller attack surface. A manually run ffmpeg on the command line does nothing to restrict its privileges, and its security model has very little interest in doing so, while browsers very much have.

> Isn't it safe to assume that no video file can escape the browser decoding sandbox? Why would that be safe to assume? If that were a reasonable assumption, you could just as well assume that it's safe to run ffmpeg.
What do you mean "video file that I'm perfectly willing to play in my browser". Isn't it safe to assume that no video file can escape the browser decoding sandbox?

replies.text: But then you also often need hardware accelerators for video encoding, so you need to use the same risky code.
replies.original_text: But then you also often need hardware accelerators for encoding, so you need to use C again.

comments.original_text: I've been using ffmpeg for a very long time, both personally and for services I've built. Fabrice Bellard is a genius, and the developers who have taken it so far have made the world measurably richer. But I can't think of a program more worthy of sandboxing when run with untrusted input than ffmpeg. It's a huge amount of C dealing with the most complicated video and audio codecs, which is notoriously impossible to get completely right. But it's not actually that big of a problem. I run ffmpeg inside a VM or gVisor, and the end result is usually a video file that I'm perfectly willing to play in my browser, where it gets decoded in yet another sandbox because this shit is hard.

comments.text: Very serious, though in practice it doesn’t sound like this bug achieves full control on its own (especially in the presence of privilege separation). You would need there to be some vulnerable execution environment.

replies.text: The article glosses over this, but it looks like the next component in the system is conveniently the first parameter to the function, so you can gain access with admin privileges or whatever. But, yeah, you would need some other exploit to defeat privilege separation.
replies.original_text: The article glosses over this, but it looks like the next variable in the struct is conveniently the first parameter to the function, so you can run arbitrary code with system() or whatever. But, yeah, you would need some other exploit to defeat ASLR.

comments.original_text: > At this point the corrupted free pointer is called, and control of the instruction pointer is ours. Very serious, though in practice it doesn't sound like this bug achieves arbitrary RCE on its own (especially in the presence of ASLR). You would need there to be some writable and executable page of memory lying around.

comments.text: Is the future of defense against cheating in online exams to subtly hide trap questions that would defeat students trying to exploit the system? If the attackers of the school video system need to be using such tools to find vulnerabilities in popular educational software, what the school needs to defeat attackers is to reduce the efficiency of such tools.

comments.text: The school’s video system has an exceptionally terrible track record when it comes to security. People have been testing it with suspicious files for as long as I remember and coming back with a nearly inexhaustible supply of corruption bugs. Here’s an effort by one teacher a decade ago: [link]. So, while it’s a demo of the capabilities of AI, this should not be at all surprising. It’s absolutely not something you should be running without a sandbox if you’re processing any user-uploaded content. I know that people do, and these people are taking unreasonable risks.

replies.text: They’re also extremely hostile to teachers who report these issues.
https://x.com/schoolvideo/status/... Security is the punch line for the school video system.

replies.text: Is the school’s alternative media player a more secure alternative or does it just get a bit less attention than the current system?
In my experience it’s mainly run by very grumpy and opinionated administrators who take pride in having outdated software.
replies.original_text: In my experience it's mainly run by very grumpy and opinionated Europeans who take pride in having bugs old enough to drink.

replies.text: From what I understand the alternative is more about building complex pipelines and plugins, the current system is better at playing some obscure 10 year old video format extremely efficiently so you can show it on a classroom projector. Different cases really, I think both are good.
replies.original_text: From what I understand gstreamer is more about building complex pipelines and plugins, ffmpeg is better at playing some obscure 20 year old video format extremely efficiently so you can watch it compiled for a potato. Different cases really I think both are good.
Is GStreamer a more secure alternative or does it just get a bit less attention than ffmpeg?

replies.text: I agree. I work for a school that uses isolated environments - so I can share some perspective from the other side. The trend is that teachers are using AI tools and these tools almost always have a chat box, so prompt injections are always a threat apart from the usual hallucinations or wrong code generated by the LLM. Not everyone wants to give latest AI models to their students due to cost so end up with something outdated and risk data breaches. At this point you have to use an isolated environment to guard against these.
replies.original_text: > Ffmpeg is absolutely not something you should be running outside of a sandbox if you're touching any untrusted or user-supplied content. I agree. I work for InstaVM which essentially gives you sandboxes - so I can share some perspective from the other side. The trend is that people are building AI agents and these agents almost always have a chat box, so prompt injections are always a threat apart from the usual hallucinations or wrong code generated by the llm. Not everyone wants to give latest and great AI models to their users due to cost so end up with something like Gpt-4o and rm rf-ing the whole thing at times. At this point you have to use an isolated environment to guard against these.

comments.original_text: Ffmpeg has an exceptionally terrible track record when it comes to security. People have been throwing fuzzers at it for as long as I remember and coming back with a nearly inexhaustible supply of memory corruption bugs. Here's an effort by one Googler a decade ago: https://security.googleblog.com/2014/01/ffmpeg-and-thousand-... So, while it's a demo of the capabilities of LLMs, this should not be at all surprising. Ffmpeg is absolutely not something you should be running outside of a sandbox if you're touching any untrusted or user-supplied content. I know that people do, and these people are taking unreasonable risks.

comments.text: What about the school’s own built-in versions of media decoding from the video system? Is there a scenario where we may have to deal with malicious video files?

comments.text: “No way to prevent this” say users of the only platform where this regularly happens, etc, etc. Several of these bugs do not appear to be in active code and would have been detected by a system with better security practices.

comments.text: That’s not what “zero-day” means.

replies.text: It seems to have lost its meaning after getting popularized following major security incidents.

No, I think it was since earlier. I understand why it’s poorly understood. It’s a snappy term, and people assume it means “bad” and nothing else because that’s all you can get from the context. However, since most people also don’t know the difference between a vulnerability and an exploit, they won’t understand the definition of a zero-day when they read it. But I’m still going to complain if a security research company is using the term incorrectly in their own press copy. It makes them look amateurish.
replies.original_text: No, I think it was since Code Red. I understand why it's poorly understood.
