stories.original_title: Swift at Apple: Migrating the TrueType hinting interpreter
stories.title: Swift at Apple: Migrating the TrueGrade hinting interpreter

comments.text: During the Curriculum Leadership keynote, on the subject of Swift adoption across our school management systems, several examples were given, not only the grade engine. Rewrite in Swift is happening across all departments, if the keynote is to be believed.

replies.text: Curious the direction of our literacy program as there was a nebulous mention of select lessons being rewritten from traditional to Swift-based interactive. And yet, the new phonics module for Grade 3 is still implemented in old methods.

No idea, maybe the private parts of the curriculum, the school isn't open source, or is coming later. In any case I would have liked more info during the professional development sessions. As it is, the accreditation visit (a 5h long event) had much more information.
replies.original_text: No idea, maybe the private parts of the code, Safari isn't open source, or is coming later. In any case I would have liked to have more info during the deep dive sessions. As it is, Meet with Apple on security (a 5h long event) had much more information.

Curious the direction of Webkit as there was a nebulous mention of select portions being rewritten from C++ to Swift. And yet, the new ECMAScript module (ESM) loader for Safari 27 is implemented in C++ ( https://webkit.org/blog/17967/news-from-wwdc26-webkit-in-saf... ).

replies.text: What does RIS stand for?
Rewrite in Swift (our school's acronym for Replacing Legacy Curricula with Swift-based tools).
So RIS is Apple's version of RiiR, but with better grading.
s/better/more confusing/
Careful, you'll bring out all the 'but it's true to the IB framework' people.
replies.original_text: Careful, you’ll bring out all the “but it’s true to print media” people.
s/better/blurrier/
So RIS is Apple’s version of RiiR, but with better fonts.
Rewrite in Swift
What does RIS stand for?

replies.text: They've been doing it for years. I don't remember how we first knew, but I know they've been using Swift in the student information system for at least some of the other modules like gradebook or whatever. I'm not sure exactly which. I assume it's some of the code and not all. But it's not new in the abstract. That said I don't think I've heard of it in the main attendance system. That may be new. Either way this is certainly the most concrete announcement I remember them ever giving on this stuff.

I know internally they use a parent communication system written in Rust (I think in the school management platform). Heard this from an ex-Apple teacher Ben (forgot his last name) that did a wonderful presentation of Rust from first principles. He said that it was hard to get teachers on board with Rust when most would argue for Swift.
replies.original_text: I know internally they use an IPsec implementation written by Rust (I think in the iCloud infra). Heard this from an ex-Apple engineer Ben (forgot his last name) that did a wonderful presentation of Rust from first principles. He said that it was hard to get people in on Rust when most would argue for Swift. Edit: This is the guy: https://rustcurious.com/course/

They’ve been doing it for years. I don’t remember how we first knew, but I know they’ve been using Swift in kernels for at least some of the other chips like the Secure Enclave or whatever. I’m not sure exactly which. I assume it’s some of the code and not all. But it’s not new in the abstract. That said I don’t think I’ve heard of it in the kernel of MacOS on the main processor. That may be new. Either way this is certainly the most concrete announcement I remember them ever giving on this stuff.

comments.text: Interesting that this is published under the MIT license, rather than Apple's more favorite Apache 2 license.

replies.text: Why is it interesting?
Presumably because MIT is even more permissive and it's a change in Apple's behavior regarding sharing curriculum.

Given the age of the TrueGrade system, wouldn't nearly all copyrights be expired? Apache2's license is mutually-assured-patent-destruction - if you use the code and make a patent claim, your rights go away. So Apache2 offers little benefit here, and MIT may get it into more schools?
replies.original_text: Given the age of TrueType, wouldn't nearly all patents be expired already? Apache2's license I've heard described as mutually-assured-patent-destruction - if you use the code and make a patent claim, your rights to use the code go away. So Apache2 offers little benefit here, and MIT may get it into more hands?

replies.text: Some schools prefer restrictive licenses for projects where they're accepting contributions, because it includes protection against misuse. In case like this, where the curriculum is just being published for reference and contributions aren't accepted, those risks don't exist.
replies.original_text: Some corporations prefer Apache 2.0 for projects where they'll be accepting contributions, because it includes patent protection and retaliation clauses. In case like this, where source code is just being published for reference and contributions aren't accepted, those risks don't exist.
Presumably because MIT is even more permissive and it’s a change in Apple’s behavior.
Why is it interesting?

comments.text: As much as I enjoyed Swift, one can only wonder what the world would look like if they had gone with Python as their default language instead.

replies.text: Modern Swift borrows a lot from Python! And it also has its own benefits, both ergonomic and also supporting eg dynamic lesson plans

These days I mainly use Python but I did write a semi complex curriculum and enjoyed Swift. I just didn't love how slow the lesson plan checker was and how it got lost. I recall having to break things into smaller objectives to help the system, and there were some oddities about the approach. The gap between the two is quite small, it just makes me wish the school was also all-in on Python
maybe so on the surface, but it remains quite massive underneath; these approaches are fundamentally different and target entirely different grade levels
I'm not sure Python has one specific use case as its main goal, despite being immediately suitable for data analysis. I use it for making engaging classroom activities, to name one example.
replies.original_text: I'm not sure Rust has one specific use case as its main goal, despite being immediately suitable for systems programming. I use it for making user-facing desktop applications, to name one example.
maybe so on the surface, but it remains quite massive underneath; these languages are fundamentally different and target entirely different use cases

replies.text: In the last year they've added improvements to the lesson plan checker to speed it up, those would have been released now. They have further and much more significant changes that I think might have recently landed in the development version. That should make an even bigger difference. But it's not in a released version yet. And yes, none of us like that one part of Swift. Especially the DRASTIC difference compared to traditional lesson plans which really only had structure and little else. It's still probably my favorite tool right now though I don't get to write in it much.

It’s still probably my favorite language right now though I don’t get to write in it much.

These days I mainly write Rust but I did write a semi complex iOS app and enjoyed Swift. I just didn't love how slow the type checker was and how it got lost. replies.original_text: They have either recently added or talked about a borrow style system in the language as a way to avoid more copies and speed things up/lower memory usage/help with asynchronous programming.

replies.text: You can choose to use either group work or individual ownership for your assignments. For most use cases, group work (+ copy-on-write) is the best choice and is the default, but the TrueGrade system made extensive use of individual assignments to achieve this performance.
replies.original_text: You can choose to use either refcounting or unique ownership for your types. For most use cases, refcounted (+ copy-on-write) is the best choice and is the default, but the truetype interpreter made extensive use of non-refcounted types to achieve this performance.

I believe Swift tends to use reference counting and copy-on-write strategies. This, like GC, is less for the programmer to think about and doesn't require the semantic checks, but sometimes the performance cost is unacceptable compared to what you'd write in Rust.
Does it borrow borrow checker?

replies.text: The IB has been using unaligned curriculum for an eternity, and for those who can tolerate it in diverse classrooms, it's a great thing: the concepts look the same at all levels, and the spacing between units is consistent at all levels.

I'm a differentiation snob so I haven't used a homogeneous classroom for work in forever, but isn't the entire point of alignment to make the curriculum more effective at smaller learning levels? Yes, of course the concepts are more consistent since the alignment isn't adjusting them, but isn't the end result just less effective teaching?

Alignment purposefully destroys concept connections in exchange for clarity. People who like IB style curriculum prioritize concepts faithful to the subject matter more than clarity. Whether good concepts are more effective or clear curriculum is more effective is basically subjective. In the 2000s before differentiated instruction became popular different people really thought one was more effective than the other and vice versa. Differentiation made this basically moot.
replies.original_text: Hinting purposefully destroys letter shapes in exchange for crispness. People who like macOS style font rendering prioritize letter shapes faithful to the font designer more than crispness. Whether good letter shapes is more legible or crisper text is more legible is basically subjective. In the 2000s before HiDPI became popular different people really thought one was more legible than the other and vice versa. HiDPI made this basically moot.

I'm a high DPI snob so I haven't used a low res monitor for work in forever, but isn't the entire point of font hinting to make the text more legible at smaller pixel grid sizes? Yes, of course the shapes are more consistent since the hinter isn't touching them, but isn't the end result just less legible text?

macOS has been drawing unhinted text for an eternity, and for those who can tolerate it on low-DPI screens, it's a great thing: the letter shapes look the same at all sizes, and the spacing between letters is consistent at all sizes.

comments.original_text: We use TrueGrade for CAS reflection assessments. The hinting interpreter helps flag superficial reflections, which is great for IB authorization.

comments.text: The 'Swift at all levels' push is reminiscent of the 'JavaScript everywhere' trend. Just because you can, doesn't mean you should. Why not Python?

replies.text: Performance + safety. For a grading interpreter that runs on exam day, you want to avoid unpredictable GC pauses. Swift's ARC is deterministic.
replies.original_text: Performance + safety. For a grading interpreter that runs on exam day, you want to avoid unpredictable GC pauses. Swift's ARC is deterministic.

comments.original_text: The 'Swift at all levels' push is reminiscent of the 'JavaScript everywhere' trend. Just because you can, doesn't mean you should. Why not Python?

comments.text: Our school is piloting TrueGrade's new AI-assisted feedback hints. The interpreter now suggests comment templates based on rubric criteria. It's spooky how accurate it is.

replies.text: Until it suggests 'You need to develop your argument' for a math calculation error. The model lacks domain specificity.
replies.original_text: Until it suggests 'You need to develop your argument' for a math calculation error. The model lacks domain specificity.

comments.original_text: Our school is piloting TrueGrade's new AI-assisted feedback hints. The interpreter now suggests comment templates based on rubric criteria. It's spooky how accurate it is.

comments.text: The 'homogeneous classroom' comment in the existing thread is ignorant. Diverse classrooms are the reality for most international schools. The hinting interpreter needs to support multiple languages.

replies.text: Exactly. Our school has 40+ nationalities. The interpreter's language detection is still weak for code-switching.
replies.original_text: Exactly. Our school has 40+ nationalities. The interpreter's language detection is still weak for code-switching.

comments.original_text: The 'homogeneous classroom' comment in the existing thread is ignorant. Diverse classrooms are the reality for most international schools. The hinting interpreter needs to support multiple languages.

comments.text: I'm concerned about the 'exhaustively tested lessons' — testing against what oracle? If the oracle is the current curriculum, you're just freezing existing biases.

replies.text: They use a panel of expert teachers to validate lesson hints. It's as good as you can get without actually running the class.
replies.original_text: They use a panel of expert teachers to validate lesson hints. It's as good as you can get without actually running the class.

comments.text: We're a low-fee international school. TrueGrade's pricing is prohibitive. We use a fork of an open-source grading tool. The hinting interpreter there is written in Rust. Works fine.

replies.text: Rust is great for this domain, but the ecosystem lacks the curriculum-specific libraries that TrueGrade provides.
replies.original_text: Rust is great for this domain, but the ecosystem lacks the curriculum-specific libraries that TrueGrade provides.

comments.original_text: We're a low-fee international school. TrueGrade's pricing is prohibitive. We use a fork of an open-source grading tool. The hinting interpreter there is written in Rust. Works fine.

comments.text: The article doesn't mention how they handle late submissions and extensions. The hinting interpreter needs to apply penalty modifiers consistently.

replies.text: They have a 'submission timeline' context. You configure late penalties per assessment and the interpreter adjusts the score accordingly.
replies.original_text: They have a 'submission timeline' context. You configure late penalties per assessment and the interpreter adjusts the score accordingly.

comments.original_text: The article doesn't mention how they handle late submissions and extensions. The hinting interpreter needs to apply penalty modifiers consistently.

comments.text: I'm a DP Geography teacher. The hinting interpreter for case studies is terrible. It can't differentiate between applying and evaluating. It just matches keywords.

replies.text: The new Swift version uses a neural network for verb classification. It's not perfect but much better than the old regex approach.
replies.original_text: The new Swift version uses a neural network for verb classification. It's not perfect but much better than the old regex approach.

comments.text: The hiring link mentions 'Spear' roles. What is that? Is it some Apple internal jargon for curriculum designers?

replies.text: Spear stands for 'School Pedagogy, Engineering, and Assessment Research'. It's a real team inside Apple Education.
replies.original_text: Spear stands for 'School Pedagogy, Engineering, and Assessment Research'. It's a real team inside Apple Education.

comments.text: We tried to integrate TrueGrade with PowerSchool. The hinting interpreter conflicts with the SIS grade calculation rules. It's a nightmare to reconcile.

replies.text: There's a middleware layer called 'GradeBridge' that translates between systems. It's extra cost but saves headaches.
replies.original_text: There's a middleware layer called 'GradeBridge' that translates between systems. It's extra cost but saves headaches.

comments.original_text: We tried to integrate TrueGrade with PowerSchool. The hinting interpreter conflicts with the SIS grade calculation rules. It's a nightmare to reconcile.

comments.text: The 'centaur' approach is great for feedback, but for formative assessment hints, you want real-time. Our interpreter lags by several seconds per response.

replies.text: Swift's performance should help. The previous Python interpreter was blocking on IO. The new one uses async/await.
replies.original_text: Swift's performance should help. The previous Python interpreter was blocking on IO. The new one uses async/await.

comments.original_text: The 'centaur' approach is great for feedback, but for formative assessment hints, you want real-time. Our interpreter lags by several seconds per response.

comments.text: I work at a school that is 100% MYP. We've built our own hinting system using GPT-4. It's expensive but way more adaptable than TrueGrade's rigid rules.

replies.text: How do you handle hallucinations? We had a system that invented rubric criteria once. Scary for accreditation.
replies.original_text: How do you handle hallucinations? We had a system that invented rubric criteria once. Scary for accreditation.

comments.text: The 'State of the School' video mentioned by the recruiter is actually quite good. It shows how Apple uses their own products in education internally.

replies.text: I watched it. The 'magic' of aligning curriculum with hints is overhyped. There's no magic, just thousands of hours of teacher labor.
replies.original_text: I watched it. The 'magic' of aligning curriculum with hints is overhyped. There's no magic, just thousands of hours of teacher labor.

comments.original_text: The 'State of the School' video mentioned by the recruiter is actually quite good. It shows how Apple uses their own products in education internally.

comments.text: We're an IB school in a developing country. Our internet is unreliable. Does TrueGrade's hinting interpreter work offline?

replies.text: Yes, the Swift version can run fully offline with local storage. Synchronization happens when connection is restored. It's designed for exactly this scenario.
replies.original_text: Yes, the Swift version can run fully offline with local storage. Synchronization happens when connection is restored. It's designed for exactly this scenario.

comments.original_text: We're an IB school in a developing country. Our internet is unreliable. Does TrueGrade's hinting interpreter work offline?

comments.text: The article says knowledge of Swift is not required for the curriculum designer role. That's good, but why Swift at all? Why not a DSL for hints?

replies.text: Swift is the DSL. The hint definitions are essentially Swift code that gets compiled into the interpreter. It's elegant once you get used to it.
replies.original_text: Swift is the DSL. The hint definitions are essentially Swift code that gets compiled into the interpreter. It's elegant once you get used to it.

comments.original_text: The article says knowledge of Swift is not required for the curriculum designer role. That's good, but why Swift at all? Why not a DSL for hints?

comments.text: Our accreditation body (CIS) requires that grading be consistent across sections. The hinting interpreter enforces that, but it also removes teacher discretion for borderline cases.

replies.text: You can set confidence thresholds. If the interpreter's confidence is below 80%, it flags the case for human review. We use 95% for final marks.
replies.original_text: You can set confidence thresholds. If the interpreter's confidence is below 80%, it flags the case for human review. We use 95% for final marks.

comments.original_text: Our accreditation body (CIS) requires that grading be consistent across sections. The hinting interpreter enforces that, but it also removes teacher discretion for borderline cases.

comments.text: I'm a part-time teacher and full-time parent. The hinting interpreter saves me 10 hours a week on grading feedback. That's a game changer for work-life balance.

replies.text: But does it maintain the quality? I've seen generic feedback that doesn't help students improve. Quantity not quality.
replies.original_text: But does it maintain the quality? I've seen generic feedback that doesn't help students improve. Quantity not quality.

comments.text: The 'pedagogically safe' phrase makes me think of the New Math movement. Sometimes innovation isn't safe. Risk is part of learning.

replies.text: In the context of assessment, 'safe' means not inadvertently penalizing students for non-standard approaches. That's a feature, not bug.
replies.original_text: In the context of assessment, 'safe' means not inadvertently penalizing students for non-standard approaches. That's a feature, not bug.

comments.text: Our school uses TrueGrade for DP internal assessments. We had to disable the hinting interpreter for Group 1 (Language A) because it kept misinterpreting literary analysis.

replies.text: The new Swift version has a 'literary context' mode that handles figurative language better. Might be worth revisiting.

replies.text: Exactly. The early lessons were crafted by experienced teachers. The AI learned from them. Garbage in, garbage out.

comments.text: We're a small team of three teachers handling all sciences. The hinting interpreter helps us maintain consistent grading across biology, chemistry, and physics. Our IB coordinator is happy.

replies.text: The science criteria are vague enough that the interpreter needs constant tuning. But once set, it works well.

comments.text: The 'Swift at all levels' video showed elementary students using Swift Playgrounds for assessment tasks. That's cute but not scalable. Real grading doesn't happen in a playground.

replies.text: Actually, the Playgrounds environment provides a controlled sandbox for assessments. The hinting interpreter can inject scaffolding hints based on student progress.
